You said: makke me crazy good in sqlplus first is there a command to see the command execution of a query

makke me crazy good in sqlplus first is there a command to see the command execution of a query

11:38 AM

Claude responded: Great question — this is fundamental to becoming a power user in SQL\*Plus.

Great question — this is fundamental to becoming a power user in SQL*Plus. Yes, there is, and it goes deeper than most people realize.

The command is EXPLAIN PLAN. Here's what's actually happening under the hood.

What EXPLAIN PLAN does at the engine level

When you run a query in Oracle, the query optimizer has to decide how to retrieve the data — which indexes to use, in what order to join tables, whether to scan the full table or hop through an index. EXPLAIN PLAN makes Oracle generate that execution plan and write it to a table called PLAN_TABLE, without actually running the query. It's like asking a taxi driver to plan the route before you get in the car.

sql
EXPLAIN PLAN FOR
SELECT * FROM employees WHERE department_id = 10;

Then you read the plan out of PLAN_TABLE using this:

sql
SELECT * FROM TABLE(DBMS_XPLAN.DISPLAY);

DBMS_XPLAN.DISPLAY is a pipelined function that formats the raw rows in PLAN_TABLE into a human-readable tree. Without it, PLAN_TABLE is just a mess of OPERATION, OPTIONS, OBJECT_NAME columns that are hard to parse.

What the output actually means

Think of a supermarket supply chain. The optimizer decides — do I:

  • Go to every shelf and check every item? (Full table scan — slow, but sometimes unavoidable)
  • Use the barcode catalog to go directly to the shelf? (Index scan — fast)
  • First sort by supplier, then by product? (Hash join vs nested loop join — depends on data volume)

The output looks like this:

Plan hash value: 1234567890

------------------------------------------------------------------
| Id  | Operation         | Name      | Rows  | Bytes | Cost    |
------------------------------------------------------------------
|   0 | SELECT STATEMENT  |           |     1 |    20 |       2 |
|   1 |  TABLE ACCESS FULL| EMPLOYEES |     1 |    20 |       2 |
------------------------------------------------------------------

Key columns to understand:

  • Operation — what Oracle is doing (TABLE ACCESS, INDEX SCAN, HASH JOIN, SORT, etc.)
  • Name — which table or index is being used
  • Rows — how many rows Oracle estimates will be processed at that step (from statistics)
  • Cost — Oracle's internal cost unit (not seconds — it's a relative number based on I/O + CPU estimates)
  • Bytes — estimated data volume moved at that step

The tree structure is indented — inner operations feed into outer ones, bottom-up.

The deeper option: SET AUTOTRACE

EXPLAIN PLAN gives you the predicted plan. SET AUTOTRACE gives you both the predicted plan and actual runtime statistics after the query executes. Like the difference between a weather forecast and the actual weather report.

sql
SET AUTOTRACE ON
SELECT * FROM employees WHERE department_id = 10;

This runs the query and shows:

  1. The query results
  2. The execution plan
  3. Statistics block — actual logical reads, physical reads, sorts, rows processed
Statistics
----------------------------------------------------------
          0  recursive calls
          0  db block gets
          8  consistent gets       ← logical I/O (buffer cache reads)
          0  physical reads        ← actual disk reads (0 = all cached)
          0  redo size
        ...
          1  rows processed

consistent gets is the most important number here. It tells you how many Oracle data blocks were read from the buffer cache. Think of it like page flips in a filing cabinet — even in-memory reads have a cost at scale. A query doing 1 million consistent gets when you expected 10 is a signal something's wrong.

You can also do:

sql
SET AUTOTRACE TRACEONLY    -- shows plan + stats, but suppresses actual output
SET AUTOTRACE TRACEONLY EXPLAIN  -- only shows the plan, runs nothing

Let me make this visual so you can see how a plan actually flows:

V

visualize

V

visualize show_widget

The critical thing most people miss: plans are estimated, not real

EXPLAIN PLAN uses table statistics (gathered by DBMS_STATS) to estimate row counts. If your stats are stale, the plan might look completely different from what actually runs. The Rows column is Oracle's guess. If Oracle thinks a WHERE clause returns 10 rows but it actually returns 10 million, you'll see a catastrophically wrong join method chosen.

To see what Oracle actually did at runtime (not estimated), you need the real execution plan from the shared SQL area:

sql
SELECT * FROM TABLE(DBMS_XPLAN.DISPLAY_CURSOR(NULL, NULL, 'ALLSTATS LAST'));

This requires the query to already be in the buffer and needs STATISTICS_LEVEL=ALL or the /*+ GATHER_PLAN_STATISTICS */ hint. That's the next depth level.

Quick reference — three levels of inspection:

CommandRuns query?What you get
EXPLAIN PLAN FOR ...NoEstimated plan only
SET AUTOTRACE TRACEONLY EXPLAINNoFormatted plan only
SET AUTOTRACE ONYesPlan + actual runtime stats
DISPLAY_CURSORAlready ranReal plan with actual row counts

Start with SET AUTOTRACE ON for day-to-day use — it's the most practical. What do you want to tackle next: understanding join types in plans (nested loop vs hash vs merge), or how indexes show up in plans?

You said: is there a docker image to run sql server i was usin it but forgot its name

is there a docker image to run sql server i was usin it but forgot its name

11:47 AM

Claude responded: The image is mcr.

Searched the web

The image is mcr.microsoft.com/mssql/server — hosted on Microsoft's own container registry (MCR), not Docker Hub. Docker Hub

Quick run command:

bash
docker run -e 'ACCEPT_EULA=Y' \
  -e 'MSSQL_SA_PASSWORD=YourStrong!Pass1' \
  -p 1433:1433 \
  -d mcr.microsoft.com/mssql/server:2022-latest

Two things to remember about the password — it needs at least 8 characters from at least 3 of these 4 categories: uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and non-alphanumeric symbols. Something like YourStrong!Pass1 works. Docker Hub

Available tags:

TagUse case
2022-latestSQL Server 2022 (recommended)
2019-latestSQL Server 2019
2022-CU23-ubuntu-22.04Pinned to a specific CU release

To connect into the container with sqlcmd:

bash
docker exec -it <container_id> \
  /opt/mssql-tools/bin/sqlcmd -S localhost -U sa -P 'YourStrong!Pass1'

One heads-up for your setup specifically — SQL Server container images are supported only on Linux hosts running on Intel and AMD x86-64 CPUs. Your Ryzen 7 7730U is x86-64 so you're good, it'll run fine. Microsoft Learn

11:48 AM

Claude responded: That's likely sqlcmd or more probably Azure Data Studio — but with an "x" specifically, you're probably thinking of:

That's likely sqlcmd or more probably Azure Data Studio — but with an "x" specifically, you're probably thinking of:

mcr.microsoft.com/mssql/server isn't it — you might mean sql-server-linux (old name) or the tool DBeaver... actually the most likely candidate is:

dbxtra? No — almost certainly you mean SQL Server Express edition, which you'd run with:

bash
docker run -e 'ACCEPT_EULA=Y' \
  -e 'MSSQL_SA_PASSWORD=YourStrong!Pass1' \
  -e 'MSSQL_PID=Express' \
  -p 1433:1433 \
  -d mcr.microsoft.com/mssql/server:2022-latest

Or — if it's a GUI tool with "x" in the name, could it be TablePlus? DBngin?

Can you remember more context — was the "x" thing the Docker image name itself, or a client/GUI tool you used to connect to SQL Server?

You said: it was in the name of the image and i would just run it and start using it in sqlplus

it was in the name of the image and i would just run it and start using it in sqlplus

11:50 AM

Claude responded: That sounds like Oracle XE (Oracle Database Express Edition) — not SQL Server at all!

That sounds like Oracle XE (Oracle Database Express Edition) — not SQL Server at all! The image is:

bash
docker run -d \
  -p 1521:1521 \
  -e ORACLE_PASSWORD=yourpassword \
  gvenzl/oracle-xe

gvenzl/oracle-xe is the most popular community image for it. The "XE" stands for Express Edition — free, lightweight, and you connect to it directly with SQL*Plus, which is why it clicks — you were using Oracle all along, not SQL Server.

Connect to it once it's running:

bash
sqlplus system/yourpassword@//localhost:1521/XE

Or from inside the container:

bash
docker exec -it <container_id> sqlplus system/yourpassword

There's also the official Oracle image if you need it:

container-registry.oracle.com/database/express:latest

But gvenzl/oracle-xe is way easier — no login wall, pulls instantly, and just works. That's almost certainly what you were using before.

You said: this is it what is the default system naem and password

this is it what is the default system naem and password

11:51 AM

Claude responded: The default credentials for gvenzl/oracle-xe are:

The default credentials for gvenzl/oracle-xe are:

FieldValue
Usernamesystem
Passwordwhatever you set in -e ORACLE_PASSWORD=
ServiceXE
Port1521

There's no hardcoded default password — you must pass ORACLE_PASSWORD when running the container. So if you ran it before as:

bash
docker run -d -p 1521:1521 -e ORACLE_PASSWORD=oracle gvenzl/oracle-xe

Then connect with:

bash
sqlplus system/oracle@//localhost:1521/XE

A common convention people use for local dev is just oracle as the password since it's not production. Try that if you don't remember what you set.

If you didn't set ORACLE_PASSWORD, the image has a fallback — the default password is:

So connect with:

You said: can date type be compared in sqlplus

can date type be compared in sqlplus

12:24 PM

Claude responded: Yes, directly.

Yes, directly. In Oracle, DATE is a full datetime type (stores date + time down to the second), so you can compare with =, <, >, BETWEEN, etc.

sql
-- rows after a specific date
SELECT * FROM employees WHERE hire_date > DATE '2020-01-01';

-- between two dates
SELECT * FROM orders 
WHERE order_date BETWEEN DATE '2023-01-01' AND DATE '2023-12-31';

-- equal to today
SELECT * FROM logs WHERE log_date = TRUNC(SYSDATE);

The tricky part — time component

This is where most people get burned. DATE in Oracle stores time too, so:

sql
WHERE hire_date = DATE '2020-01-01'

This won't match a row where hire_date is 2020-01-01 09:35:00 — because the time part makes them unequal, just like 2020-01-01 00:00:00 ≠ 2020-01-01 09:35:00.

Fix it with TRUNC(), which strips the time component:

sql
WHERE TRUNC(hire_date) = DATE '2020-01-01'

Think of it like comparing two timestamps on a security log — Jan 1 midnight and Jan 1 9am are the same day but not the same moment.

Useful functions for date comparison:

sql
SYSDATE          -- current date + time
TRUNC(SYSDATE)   -- today at midnight (00:00:00)
TRUNC(d, 'MM')   -- first day of the month
TRUNC(d, 'YYYY') -- first day of the year

-- add/subtract days (Oracle dates support arithmetic)
SYSDATE + 7      -- 7 days from now
SYSDATE - 30     -- 30 days ago

So hire_date > SYSDATE - 30 just works — no special function needed for simple arithmetic.

You said: Car Rental System

Car Rental System Car (Car_ID, Brand, Model, Rent_Per_Day, Availability) Customer (Cust_ID, Cust Name, License_No, City) Rental (Rental ID, Cust ID, Car_ID, Rent_Date, Amount) 1. Display cars not rented yet. 2. Retrieve the car generating highest rental revenue. 3. Show brandwise rental count. 4. Find customers renting more than average times. 5. Demonstrate MongoDB aggregation on rentals. Create table and insert values and answer for the query

3:38 PM

Claude responded: Let's build this from scratch.

Let's build this from scratch. First let me spin up the Oracle XE container and do everything inside it.

Check if oracle-xe container is running

Docker isn't available in this sandbox, so I'll write all the SQL clearly so you can run it directly in your SQL*Plus session. Let's go step by step.

Step 1 — Create Tables

sql
CREATE TABLE Car (
    Car_ID       NUMBER PRIMARY KEY,
    Brand        VARCHAR2(50),
    Model        VARCHAR2(50),
    Rent_Per_Day NUMBER(8,2),
    Availability VARCHAR2(3) CHECK (Availability IN ('YES','NO'))
);

CREATE TABLE Customer (
    Cust_ID     NUMBER PRIMARY KEY,
    Cust_Name   VARCHAR2(100),
    License_No  VARCHAR2(50),
    City        VARCHAR2(50)
);

CREATE TABLE Rental (
    Rental_ID  NUMBER PRIMARY KEY,
    Cust_ID    NUMBER REFERENCES Customer(Cust_ID),
    Car_ID     NUMBER REFERENCES Car(Car_ID),
    Rent_Date  DATE,
    Amount     NUMBER(10,2)
);

Step 2 — Insert Data

sql
-- Cars
INSERT INTO Car VALUES (1, 'Toyota',  'Innova',  2500, 'NO');
INSERT INTO Car VALUES (2, 'Honda',   'City',    1800, 'NO');
INSERT INTO Car VALUES (3, 'Hyundai', 'Creta',   2200, 'YES');  -- never rented
INSERT INTO Car VALUES (4, 'Toyota',  'Fortuner',3500, 'NO');
INSERT INTO Car VALUES (5, 'Maruti',  'Swift',   1200, 'YES');  -- never rented
INSERT INTO Car VALUES (6, 'Honda',   'Amaze',   1500, 'NO');

-- Customers
INSERT INTO Customer VALUES (1, 'Tarun',   'TN1234', 'Madurai');
INSERT INTO Customer VALUES (2, 'Arjun',   'TN5678', 'Chennai');
INSERT INTO Customer VALUES (3, 'Priya',   'KA4321', 'Bangalore');
INSERT INTO Customer VALUES (4, 'Meena',   'TN9999', 'Madurai');

-- Rentals
INSERT INTO Rental VALUES (1,  1, 1, DATE '2024-01-05', 7500);
INSERT INTO Rental VALUES (2,  2, 2, DATE '2024-01-10', 3600);
INSERT INTO Rental VALUES (3,  1, 4, DATE '2024-02-01', 10500);
INSERT INTO Rental VALUES (4,  3, 1, DATE '2024-02-15', 5000);
INSERT INTO Rental VALUES (5,  2, 6, DATE '2024-03-01', 4500);
INSERT INTO Rental VALUES (6,  1, 2, DATE '2024-03-10', 1800);
INSERT INTO Rental VALUES (7,  4, 4, DATE '2024-03-20', 7000);
INSERT INTO Rental VALUES (8,  3, 6, DATE '2024-04-01', 3000);

COMMIT;

Query 1 — Cars not rented yet

Logic: Cars whose Car_ID never appears in the Rental table. Like finding products that have never been sold — check the invoice table, if it's absent, the product sat on the shelf untouched.

sql
SELECT c.Car_ID, c.Brand, c.Model, c.Rent_Per_Day
FROM Car c
WHERE c.Car_ID NOT IN (
    SELECT Car_ID FROM Rental
);

Output:

CAR_ID  BRAND    MODEL   RENT_PER_DAY
------  -------  ------  ------------
3       Hyundai  Creta   2200
5       Maruti   Swift   1200

You can also write this with NOT EXISTS — faster on large tables because NOT IN breaks if any subquery row is NULL.

sql
WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM Rental r WHERE r.Car_ID = c.Car_ID)

Query 2 — Car generating highest rental revenue

Logic: SUM the Amount grouped by Car_ID, then pick the top earner. Like finding which product line brought in the most sales revenue.

sql
SELECT c.Car_ID, c.Brand, c.Model, SUM(r.Amount) AS Total_Revenue
FROM Car c
JOIN Rental r ON c.Car_ID = r.Car_ID
GROUP BY c.Car_ID, c.Brand, c.Model
ORDER BY Total_Revenue DESC
FETCH FIRST 1 ROW ONLY;

Output:

CAR_ID  BRAND   MODEL     TOTAL_REVENUE
------  ------  --------  -------------
4       Toyota  Fortuner  17500

FETCH FIRST 1 ROW ONLY is Oracle 12c+ syntax. On older Oracle use:

sql
WHERE ROWNUM = 1

but wrap it around the ordered query, otherwise ROWNUM filters before ORDER BY.

Query 3 — Brand-wise rental count

Logic: GROUP BY Brand, COUNT rentals. Like a sales report showing which brand of phone sold the most units across all models.

sql
SELECT c.Brand, COUNT(r.Rental_ID) AS Rental_Count
FROM Car c
JOIN Rental r ON c.Car_ID = r.Car_ID
GROUP BY c.Brand
ORDER BY Rental_Count DESC;

Output:

BRAND    RENTAL_COUNT
-------  ------------
Honda    3
Toyota   3
Hyundai  0  -- won't appear (never rented)

If you want brands with zero rentals too, use LEFT JOIN:

sql
FROM Car c LEFT JOIN Rental r ON c.Car_ID = r.Car_ID

Query 4 — Customers renting more than average times

Logic: First find the average rental count per customer, then filter customers who exceed it. Like finding students who submitted more assignments than the class average.

sql
SELECT cu.Cust_ID, cu.Cust_Name, COUNT(r.Rental_ID) AS Rental_Count
FROM Customer cu
JOIN Rental r ON cu.Cust_ID = r.Cust_ID
GROUP BY cu.Cust_ID, cu.Cust_Name
HAVING COUNT(r.Rental_ID) > (
    SELECT AVG(cnt)
    FROM (
        SELECT COUNT(Rental_ID) AS cnt
        FROM Rental
        GROUP BY Cust_ID
    )
);

How the subquery works — inside out:

Inner: COUNT per customer  →  Tarun:3, Arjun:2, Priya:2, Meena:1
AVG of those counts        →  (3+2+2+1)/4 = 2
HAVING > 2                 →  only Tarun (3 rentals)

Output:

CUST_ID  CUST_NAME  RENTAL_COUNT
-------  ---------  ------------
1        Tarun      3

Query 5 — MongoDB aggregation equivalent

MongoDB doesn't use SQL*Plus — it's a NoSQL document database. But the question asks you to demonstrate the aggregation concept on rentals using MongoDB syntax. Here's how the same queries map:

Collection structure (each rental is a document):

json
{
  "Rental_ID": 1,
  "Cust_ID": 1,
  "Car_ID": 1,
  "Brand": "Toyota",
  "Rent_Date": "2024-01-05",
  "Amount": 7500
}

Insert documents:

javascript
db.rentals.insertMany([
  { Rental_ID:1, Cust_ID:1, Car_ID:1, Brand:"Toyota",  Amount:7500  },
  { Rental_ID:2, Cust_ID:2, Car_ID:2, Brand:"Honda",   Amount:3600  },
  { Rental_ID:3, Cust_ID:1, Car_ID:4, Brand:"Toyota",  Amount:10500 },
  { Rental_ID:4, Cust_ID:3, Car_ID:1, Brand:"Toyota",  Amount:5000  },
  { Rental_ID:5, Cust_ID:2, Car_ID:6, Brand:"Honda",   Amount:4500  },
  { Rental_ID:6, Cust_ID:1, Car_ID:2, Brand:"Honda",   Amount:1800  },
  { Rental_ID:7, Cust_ID:4, Car_ID:4, Brand:"Toyota",  Amount:7000  },
  { Rental_ID:8, Cust_ID:3, Car_ID:6, Brand:"Honda",   Amount:3000  }
])

Brand-wise rental count (equivalent to Query 3):

javascript
db.rentals.aggregate([
  { $group: {
      _id: "$Brand",
      Rental_Count: { $sum: 1 },
      Total_Revenue: { $sum: "$Amount" }
  }},
  { $sort: { Rental_Count: -1 } }
])

Highest revenue car (equivalent to Query 2):

javascript
db.rentals.aggregate([
  { $group: {
      _id: "$Car_ID",
      Total_Revenue: { $sum: "$Amount" }
  }},
  { $sort: { Total_Revenue: -1 } },
  { $limit: 1 }
])

The pipeline concept — think of it like an assembly line. Each $stage takes documents in, transforms them, passes them to the next stage. $group is like Oracle's GROUP BY, $sort is ORDER BY, $limit is FETCH FIRST N ROWS.

Run the SQL blocks in order in your SQL*Plus session — CREATE → INSERT → COMMIT → then each query. Want me to go deeper on any of these queries?

You said: Electricity Billing System

Electricity Billing System Consumer (Cons_ID, Cons_Name, City, Meter_No) Bill (Bill_ID, Cons_ID, Bill_Month, Units_Used, Amount) 1. Display consumers with unpaid bills. 2. Retrieve the consumer with highest electricity usage. 3. Show monthwise electricity revenue. 4. Find citywise average consumption. 5. Write a PL/SQL block for Bill table operations.

4:13 PM

Claude responded: Let me build this fully — tables, data, all 5 queries, and a proper PL/SQL block.

Let me build this fully — tables, data, all 5 queries, and a proper PL/SQL block.

Step 1 — Create Tables

sql
CREATE TABLE Consumer (
    Cons_ID   NUMBER PRIMARY KEY,
    Cons_Name VARCHAR2(100),
    City      VARCHAR2(50),
    Meter_No  VARCHAR2(20) UNIQUE
);

CREATE TABLE Bill (
    Bill_ID    NUMBER PRIMARY KEY,
    Cons_ID    NUMBER REFERENCES Consumer(Cons_ID),
    Bill_Month VARCHAR2(20),
    Units_Used NUMBER(10,2),
    Amount     NUMBER(10,2),
    Status     VARCHAR2(10) DEFAULT 'UNPAID'
                CHECK (Status IN ('PAID','UNPAID'))
);

Notice Status column — the question says "unpaid bills" so we need to track payment status. It's not in the original schema but it's logically mandatory. Default is UNPAID so every new bill starts unpaid until explicitly marked PAID.

Step 2 — Insert Data

sql
-- Consumers
INSERT INTO Consumer VALUES (1, 'Tarun',   'Madurai',   'MTR001');
INSERT INTO Consumer VALUES (2, 'Arjun',   'Chennai',   'MTR002');
INSERT INTO Consumer VALUES (3, 'Priya',   'Bangalore', 'MTR003');
INSERT INTO Consumer VALUES (4, 'Meena',   'Madurai',   'MTR004');
INSERT INTO Consumer VALUES (5, 'Karthik', 'Chennai',   'MTR005');

-- Bills
INSERT INTO Bill VALUES (1,  1, 'JAN-2024', 320,  1280, 'PAID');
INSERT INTO Bill VALUES (2,  1, 'FEB-2024', 410,  1640, 'UNPAID');
INSERT INTO Bill VALUES (3,  2, 'JAN-2024', 150,   600, 'PAID');
INSERT INTO Bill VALUES (4,  2, 'FEB-2024', 200,   800, 'UNPAID');
INSERT INTO Bill VALUES (5,  3, 'JAN-2024', 530,  2120, 'UNPAID');
INSERT INTO Bill VALUES (6,  3, 'FEB-2024', 490,  1960, 'UNPAID');
INSERT INTO Bill VALUES (7,  4, 'JAN-2024', 280,  1120, 'PAID');
INSERT INTO Bill VALUES (8,  4, 'FEB-2024', 310,  1240, 'PAID');
INSERT INTO Bill VALUES (9,  5, 'JAN-2024', 670,  2680, 'UNPAID');
INSERT INTO Bill VALUES (10, 5, 'FEB-2024', 720,  2880, 'UNPAID');

COMMIT;

Query 1 — Consumers with unpaid bills

Logic: Like a bank's defaulter list — find every account that has at least one overdue payment. We JOIN Consumer with Bill and filter on Status = 'UNPAID'. We use DISTINCT because one consumer can have multiple unpaid bills and we don't want them listed twice.

sql
SELECT DISTINCT c.Cons_ID, c.Cons_Name, c.City, c.Meter_No
FROM Consumer c
JOIN Bill b ON c.Cons_ID = b.Cons_ID
WHERE b.Status = 'UNPAID';

Output:

CONS_ID  CONS_NAME  CITY       METER_NO
-------  ---------  ---------  --------
1        Tarun      Madurai    MTR001
2        Arjun      Chennai    MTR002
3        Priya      Bangalore  MTR003
5        Karthik    Chennai    MTR005

Meena is absent because both her bills are PAID.

Query 2 — Consumer with highest electricity usage

Logic: SUM all units per consumer across all months (like totalling a customer's annual spend), then pick the top one.

sql
SELECT c.Cons_ID, c.Cons_Name, c.City, SUM(b.Units_Used) AS Total_Units
FROM Consumer c
JOIN Bill b ON c.Cons_ID = b.Cons_ID
GROUP BY c.Cons_ID, c.Cons_Name, c.City
ORDER BY Total_Units DESC
FETCH FIRST 1 ROW ONLY;

Output:

CONS_ID  CONS_NAME  CITY     TOTAL_UNITS
-------  ---------  -------  -----------
5        Karthik    Chennai  1390

Query 3 — Month-wise electricity revenue

Logic: GROUP BY Bill_Month, SUM the Amount. Like a monthly sales report — how much did the whole grid earn in January vs February.

sql
SELECT Bill_Month,
       SUM(Amount)     AS Total_Revenue,
       SUM(Units_Used) AS Total_Units,
       COUNT(Bill_ID)  AS Bills_Generated
FROM Bill
GROUP BY Bill_Month
ORDER BY Bill_Month;

Output:

BILL_MONTH  TOTAL_REVENUE  TOTAL_UNITS  BILLS_GENERATED
----------  -------------  -----------  ---------------
FEB-2024    8520           2130         5
JAN-2024    7800           1950         5

Query 4 — City-wise average consumption

Logic: GROUP BY City, AVG the Units_Used per bill. Like finding which district of a city uses more power on average — helps the electricity board plan load distribution.

sql
SELECT c.City,
       ROUND(AVG(b.Units_Used), 2) AS Avg_Units,
       COUNT(b.Bill_ID)             AS Total_Bills,
       SUM(b.Units_Used)            AS Total_Units
FROM Consumer c
JOIN Bill b ON c.Cons_ID = b.Cons_ID
GROUP BY c.City
ORDER BY Avg_Units DESC;

Output:

CITY       AVG_UNITS  TOTAL_BILLS  TOTAL_UNITS
---------  ---------  -----------  -----------
Chennai    435        4            1740
Bangalore  510        2            1020   -- wait, Priya alone, high usage
Madurai    330        4            1320

AVG is per-bill not per-consumer, so a city with one heavy user and few bills will show high average. That's statistically correct — same way one luxury apartment skews the average rent of a street.

Query 5 — PL/SQL block for Bill table operations

This block does 4 things:

  • Inserts a new bill
  • Calculates amount automatically based on units (slab pricing like real TNEB)
  • Updates payment status
  • Prints a consumer's full bill summary using a cursor
sql
DECLARE
    -- Variables for new bill
    v_cons_id    Bill.Cons_ID%TYPE    := 3;
    v_month      Bill.Bill_Month%TYPE := 'MAR-2024';
    v_units      Bill.Units_Used%TYPE := 460;
    v_amount     Bill.Amount%TYPE;
    v_bill_id    Bill.Bill_ID%TYPE    := 11;

    -- Variables for summary
    v_name       Consumer.Cons_Name%TYPE;
    v_total      NUMBER := 0;
    v_unpaid_amt NUMBER := 0;

    -- Cursor to fetch all bills of a consumer
    CURSOR c_bills IS
        SELECT b.Bill_ID, b.Bill_Month, b.Units_Used, b.Amount, b.Status
        FROM Bill b
        WHERE b.Cons_ID = v_cons_id
        ORDER BY b.Bill_Month;

    -- Record type to hold each cursor row
    r_bill c_bills%ROWTYPE;

BEGIN
    -- ── STEP 1: Calculate amount using slab pricing ──────────────────
    -- Real TNEB style: first 100 units free, next 200 at 3.5/unit, rest at 5/unit
    IF v_units <= 100 THEN
        v_amount := 0;
    ELSIF v_units <= 300 THEN
        v_amount := (v_units - 100) * 3.5;
    ELSE
        v_amount := (200 * 3.5) + ((v_units - 300) * 5);
    END IF;

    DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('--- Calculated Amount for ' || v_units || ' units: Rs.' || v_amount);

    -- ── STEP 2: Insert new bill ──────────────────────────────────────
    INSERT INTO Bill (Bill_ID, Cons_ID, Bill_Month, Units_Used, Amount, Status)
    VALUES (v_bill_id, v_cons_id, v_month, v_units, v_amount, 'UNPAID');

    DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('--- New bill inserted: Bill_ID ' || v_bill_id);

    -- ── STEP 3: Mark a specific old bill as PAID ─────────────────────
    UPDATE Bill
    SET Status = 'PAID'
    WHERE Bill_ID = 5;   -- Priya's JAN-2024 bill

    DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('--- Bill_ID 5 marked as PAID');

    -- ── STEP 4: Print full bill summary using cursor ─────────────────
    SELECT Cons_Name INTO v_name
    FROM Consumer
    WHERE Cons_ID = v_cons_id;

    DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('');
    DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('========================================');
    DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(' Bill Summary for: ' || v_name);
    DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('========================================');
    DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(
        RPAD('Month',     12) ||
        RPAD('Units',     8)  ||
        RPAD('Amount',    10) ||
        'Status'
    );
    DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('----------------------------------------');

    OPEN c_bills;
    LOOP
        FETCH c_bills INTO r_bill;
        EXIT WHEN c_bills%NOTFOUND;

        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(
            RPAD(r_bill.Bill_Month,  12) ||
            RPAD(r_bill.Units_Used,  8)  ||
            RPAD(r_bill.Amount,      10) ||
            r_bill.Status
        );

        v_total := v_total + r_bill.Amount;

        IF r_bill.Status = 'UNPAID' THEN
            v_unpaid_amt := v_unpaid_amt + r_bill.Amount;
        END IF;

    END LOOP;
    CLOSE c_bills;

    DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('----------------------------------------');
    DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Total Billed  : Rs.' || v_total);
    DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Unpaid Amount : Rs.' || v_unpaid_amt);
    DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('========================================');

    COMMIT;

EXCEPTION
    WHEN DUP_VAL_ON_INDEX THEN
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('ERROR: Bill_ID already exists.');
        ROLLBACK;
    WHEN NO_DATA_FOUND THEN
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('ERROR: Consumer not found.');
        ROLLBACK;
    WHEN OTHERS THEN
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('ERROR: ' || SQLERRM);
        ROLLBACK;
END;
/

Before running, enable output:

sql
SET SERVEROUTPUT ON

Expected output:

--- Calculated Amount for 460 units: Rs.1500
--- New bill inserted: Bill_ID 11
--- Bill_ID 5 marked as PAID

========================================
 Bill Summary for: Priya
========================================
Month       Units   Amount    Status
----------------------------------------
FEB-2024    490     1960      UNPAID
JAN-2024    530     2120      PAID
MAR-2024    460     1500      UNPAID
----------------------------------------
Total Billed  : Rs.5580
Unpaid Amount : Rs.3460
========================================

Key PL/SQL concepts used:

ConceptWhat it doesReal-world analogy
%TYPEVariable borrows column's datatypeLike copy-pasting the data type contract from the table
CURSORPointer that walks through result rows one by oneLike a cashier processing a queue, one person at a time
%ROWTYPEOne variable holds an entire rowLike a tray that carries all items of one order
RPADRight-pads string for alignmentLike tab stops in a printed receipt
EXCEPTION blockCatches runtime errors gracefullyLike a try-catch — ATM doesn't crash, it shows "transaction failed"
SQLERRMOracle's error message stringThe actual error text Oracle would show you

Want me to extend this with a TRIGGER that auto-calculates the amount on INSERT, so you never have to pass Amount manually?

You said: so this is for an exam tommorow and i found the pattern each of the question falls on one of 8 patterns 1)not exist (flights without reservation) 2)highest val…

so this is for an exam tommorow and i found the pattern each of the question falls on one of 8 patterns 1)not exist (flights without reservation) 2)highest value (salseman with highest salary from payroll) 3)columnwise count (revenue based on month) 4)more than average (customer who rents car more time than the average) 5)more/less than n value(product with stocks less than 10) 6) mongodb aggregate 7)pl/sql block on a table 8)pl/sql with tcl on a table. I first want you to give a common format and answers for these and then i have some questions

6:25 PM

Claude responded: Smart pattern recognition.

Smart pattern recognition. Let me give you a reusable template for each pattern that you can slot any schema into.

Pattern 1 — NOT EXISTS (things with no related record)

The shape: Find rows in table A that have no matching rows in table B.

sql
SELECT a.*
FROM TableA a
WHERE NOT EXISTS (
    SELECT 1
    FROM TableB b
    WHERE b.fk_id = a.pk_id
);

Why NOT EXISTS over NOT IN: If any row in the subquery returns NULL, NOT IN returns nothing — silent wrong answer. NOT EXISTS is NULL-safe. Always use NOT EXISTS in exams.

Slot-in rule: Replace TableA with the "parent" table, TableB with the "child/transaction" table, match on the foreign key.

Pattern 2 — Highest value (max from aggregation)

The shape: SUM or COUNT per group, pick the top one.

sql
SELECT a.pk_id, a.name, SUM(b.value_col) AS Total
FROM TableA a
JOIN TableB b ON a.pk_id = b.fk_id
GROUP BY a.pk_id, a.name
ORDER BY Total DESC
FETCH FIRST 1 ROW ONLY;

Slot-in rule: Replace value_col with Amount/Salary/Units. Replace SUM with MAX if you want the single highest row value instead of aggregated total.

Pattern 3 — Column-wise count/sum (monthly, citywise, brandwise)

The shape: GROUP BY one column, aggregate another.

sql
SELECT grouping_col,
       COUNT(*)        AS Record_Count,
       SUM(value_col)  AS Total_Value,
       AVG(value_col)  AS Avg_Value
FROM TableB
GROUP BY grouping_col
ORDER BY grouping_col;

Slot-in rule: grouping_col = Month/City/Brand/Category. value_col = Amount/Units/Salary. Add a JOIN if the grouping column lives in a different table.

Pattern 4 — More than average (heavy users)

The shape: Count per entity, filter where count exceeds the average count.

sql
SELECT a.pk_id, a.name, COUNT(b.pk_id) AS Total
FROM TableA a
JOIN TableB b ON a.pk_id = b.fk_id
GROUP BY a.pk_id, a.name
HAVING COUNT(b.pk_id) > (
    SELECT AVG(cnt)
    FROM (
        SELECT COUNT(pk_id) AS cnt
        FROM TableB
        GROUP BY fk_id
    )
);

The 3-layer logic — memorize this:

Innermost : COUNT per entity         → {A:3, B:2, C:1}
Middle    : AVG of those counts      → (3+2+1)/3 = 2
Outer     : HAVING > that average    → only A

Pattern 5 — More/less than N value (threshold filter)

The shape: Simple WHERE on an aggregated or raw column.

sql
-- On raw column (stock < 10)
SELECT * FROM Product
WHERE Stock < 10;

-- On aggregated value (customers who spent more than 5000 total)
SELECT a.pk_id, a.name, SUM(b.value_col) AS Total
FROM TableA a
JOIN TableB b ON a.pk_id = b.fk_id
GROUP BY a.pk_id, a.name
HAVING SUM(b.value_col) > 5000;

Key rule: Raw column → WHERE. Aggregated column → HAVING. This is the most common exam mistake.

Pattern 6 — MongoDB Aggregation

The shape: Always a pipeline. Three stages cover every exam question.

javascript
db.collection.insertMany([
    { field1: val1, field2: val2, ... },
    ...
]);

// Group + Sum (like Q3 monthwise)
db.collection.aggregate([
    { $group: {
        _id: "$grouping_field",
        total: { $sum: "$value_field" },
        count: { $sum: 1 }
    }},
    { $sort: { total: -1 } }
]);

// Filter then group (like Q1 unpaid)
db.collection.aggregate([
    { $match: { status: "UNPAID" } },
    { $group: { _id: "$Cons_ID", total: { $sum: "$Amount" } } }
]);

// Top 1 (like Q2 highest)
db.collection.aggregate([
    { $group: { _id: "$Car_ID", revenue: { $sum: "$Amount" } } },
    { $sort: { revenue: -1 } },
    { $limit: 1 }
]);

3 stages to memorize: $match = WHERE, $group = GROUP BY, $sort + $limit = ORDER BY + FETCH FIRST 1.

Pattern 7 — PL/SQL block on a table

The shape: DECLARE → BEGIN → cursor loop → DBMS_OUTPUT → EXCEPTION.

sql
SET SERVEROUTPUT ON;

DECLARE
    -- 1. Variables (always use %TYPE)
    v_id        TableA.pk_id%TYPE   := 1;
    v_name      TableA.name%TYPE;
    v_total     NUMBER              := 0;

    -- 2. Cursor
    CURSOR c_data IS
        SELECT col1, col2, col3
        FROM TableB
        WHERE fk_id = v_id;

    r_data c_data%ROWTYPE;

BEGIN
    -- 3. Lookup
    SELECT name INTO v_name
    FROM TableA WHERE pk_id = v_id;

    -- 4. Insert
    INSERT INTO TableB VALUES (seq.NEXTVAL, v_id, 'VAL', 100);

    -- 5. Update
    UPDATE TableB SET col1 = 'NEW' WHERE pk_id = 99;

    -- 6. Cursor loop
    OPEN c_data;
    LOOP
        FETCH c_data INTO r_data;
        EXIT WHEN c_data%NOTFOUND;
        v_total := v_total + r_data.col3;
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(r_data.col1 || ' - ' || r_data.col3);
    END LOOP;
    CLOSE c_data;

    DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Total: ' || v_total);
    COMMIT;

EXCEPTION
    WHEN NO_DATA_FOUND THEN
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Not found');
        ROLLBACK;
    WHEN OTHERS THEN
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Error: ' || SQLERRM);
        ROLLBACK;
END;
/

Pattern 8 — PL/SQL with TCL (Transaction Control)

The shape: Same as Pattern 7 but with explicit SAVEPOINT, ROLLBACK TO, COMMIT logic — the examiner wants to see you know transactions.

sql
SET SERVEROUTPUT ON;

DECLARE
    v_id     NUMBER := 1;
    v_amt    NUMBER := 5000;
    v_exists NUMBER;
BEGIN
    -- Check before acting
    SELECT COUNT(*) INTO v_exists
    FROM TableA WHERE pk_id = v_id;

    IF v_exists = 0 THEN
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Record not found. Aborting.');
        RETURN;
    END IF;

    SAVEPOINT before_changes;       -- bookmark before risky ops

    -- Operation 1
    INSERT INTO TableB VALUES (99, v_id, 'JAN-2024', v_amt);
    DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Insert done.');

    SAVEPOINT after_insert;         -- second bookmark

    -- Operation 2 (risky)
    UPDATE TableA SET balance = balance - v_amt WHERE pk_id = v_id;
    DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Update done.');

    -- Simulate a validation check
    IF v_amt > 10000 THEN
        ROLLBACK TO after_insert;   -- undo only the update, keep insert
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Amount too high. Update rolled back.');
    ELSE
        COMMIT;                     -- everything goes through
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('All changes committed.');
    END IF;

EXCEPTION
    WHEN OTHERS THEN
        ROLLBACK TO before_changes; -- undo everything
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Fatal error: ' || SQLERRM);
END;
/

TCL keywords the examiner looks for:

KeywordWhat it does
COMMITMakes all changes permanent
ROLLBACKUndoes everything back to last commit
SAVEPOINT namePlaces a named bookmark mid-transaction
ROLLBACK TO nameUndoes only back to that bookmark, not full rollback

These 8 templates cover every variation. Now give me your questions — I'll map each one to its pattern and show you the exact answer.

You said: so now these are questions they asked for previous batch most probably these will be asked for us but if worst case happens what other patterns can you think of

so now these are questions they asked for previous batch most probably these will be asked for us but if worst case happens what other patterns can you think of

6:26 PM

Claude responded: Good thinking — cover the edge cases before the exam.

Good thinking — cover the edge cases before the exam. Let me think about what else examiners commonly throw in SQL/PL/SQL exams beyond your 8.

Pattern 9 — Second highest / Nth highest value

Shows up as "second highest salary", "third most rented car". Examiners love this because naive MAX() doesn't work.

sql
-- Second highest
SELECT MAX(value_col) AS Second_Highest
FROM TableA
WHERE value_col < (SELECT MAX(value_col) FROM TableA);

-- Nth highest (universal — works for any N)
SELECT value_col FROM (
    SELECT value_col,
           DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY value_col DESC) AS rnk
    FROM TableA
)
WHERE rnk = 2;  -- change 2 to N

DENSE_RANK vs RANK — if two people share rank 1, RANK skips rank 2 and jumps to 3. DENSE_RANK gives rank 2 to the next person. For "second highest" questions use DENSE_RANK.

Pattern 10 — Date range filter / most recent record

Shows up as "bills generated in last 30 days", "rentals in January", "most recent transaction per customer".

sql
-- Between two dates
SELECT * FROM Bill
WHERE Bill_Date BETWEEN DATE '2024-01-01' AND DATE '2024-01-31';

-- Last 30 days
SELECT * FROM Bill
WHERE Bill_Date >= SYSDATE - 30;

-- Most recent record per customer (classic exam trap)
SELECT Cust_ID, MAX(Rent_Date) AS Last_Rental
FROM Rental
GROUP BY Cust_ID;

Pattern 11 — JOINS explicitly tested (INNER, LEFT, SELF)

Sometimes the question says "show all consumers even if they have no bills" — that's a LEFT JOIN signal. Or "find employees managed by their own department head" — that's a SELF JOIN.

sql
-- LEFT JOIN: show all from left table even without match
SELECT c.Cons_Name, b.Bill_Month, b.Amount
FROM Consumer c
LEFT JOIN Bill b ON c.Cons_ID = b.Cons_ID;
-- Consumers with no bills appear with NULL in bill columns

-- SELF JOIN: table joined with itself
-- "Find employees in the same city as employee ID 3"
SELECT a.Emp_Name, a.City
FROM Employee a
JOIN Employee b ON a.City = b.City
WHERE b.Emp_ID = 3 AND a.Emp_ID != 3;

Pattern 12 — TRIGGER

Very common in PL/SQL sections. Three types examiners ask:

sql
-- BEFORE INSERT: auto-calculate a value before saving
CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER trg_calc_amount
BEFORE INSERT ON Bill
FOR EACH ROW
BEGIN
    IF :NEW.Units_Used <= 100 THEN
        :NEW.Amount := 0;
    ELSIF :NEW.Units_Used <= 300 THEN
        :NEW.Amount := (:NEW.Units_Used - 100) * 3.5;
    ELSE
        :NEW.Amount := (200 * 3.5) + ((:NEW.Units_Used - 300) * 5);
    END IF;
END;
/

-- AFTER INSERT: log every new bill into an audit table
CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER trg_audit_bill
AFTER INSERT ON Bill
FOR EACH ROW
BEGIN
    INSERT INTO Bill_Audit
    VALUES (:NEW.Bill_ID, :NEW.Cons_ID, SYSDATE, 'INSERTED');
END;
/

-- BEFORE DELETE: prevent deleting paid bills
CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER trg_protect_paid
BEFORE DELETE ON Bill
FOR EACH ROW
BEGIN
    IF :OLD.Status = 'PAID' THEN
        RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR(-20001, 'Cannot delete a paid bill.');
    END IF;
END;
/

Key syntax to memorize:

  • :NEW.col — value being inserted/updated (new value coming in)
  • :OLD.col — value before update/delete (what was there before)
  • FOR EACH ROW — fires once per affected row (row-level trigger)
  • RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR(-20001, 'msg') — custom error, number must be between -20000 and -20999

Pattern 13 — PROCEDURE and FUNCTION

Sometimes instead of an anonymous PL/SQL block they ask for a named procedure or function.

sql
-- PROCEDURE: does something, no return value
CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE proc_pay_bill(p_bill_id IN NUMBER) AS
    v_status Bill.Status%TYPE;
BEGIN
    SELECT Status INTO v_status FROM Bill WHERE Bill_ID = p_bill_id;

    IF v_status = 'PAID' THEN
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Already paid.');
    ELSE
        UPDATE Bill SET Status = 'PAID' WHERE Bill_ID = p_bill_id;
        COMMIT;
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Bill ' || p_bill_id || ' marked PAID.');
    END IF;
EXCEPTION
    WHEN NO_DATA_FOUND THEN
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Bill not found.');
END;
/

-- Call it
EXEC proc_pay_bill(5);

-- FUNCTION: does something AND returns a value
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION fn_total_units(p_cons_id IN NUMBER)
RETURN NUMBER AS
    v_total NUMBER;
BEGIN
    SELECT SUM(Units_Used) INTO v_total
    FROM Bill WHERE Cons_ID = p_cons_id;
    RETURN NVL(v_total, 0);  -- NVL handles NULL if no bills exist
END;
/

-- Call it
SELECT fn_total_units(3) FROM DUAL;

Procedure vs Function — one line: Procedure = does work. Function = does work AND returns a value you can use in a SELECT.

Pattern 14 — CURSOR with %FOUND / %ROWCOUNT / explicit attributes

Examiners sometimes ask you to use cursor attributes explicitly rather than just %NOTFOUND.

sql
DECLARE
    CURSOR c_bills IS SELECT * FROM Bill WHERE Status = 'UNPAID';
    r      c_bills%ROWTYPE;
    v_count NUMBER := 0;
BEGIN
    OPEN c_bills;
    LOOP
        FETCH c_bills INTO r;
        EXIT WHEN c_bills%NOTFOUND;

        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(r.Bill_ID || ' - Rs.' || r.Amount);
        v_count := v_count + 1;
    END LOOP;

    -- %ROWCOUNT = how many rows fetched so far
    DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Total unpaid bills: ' || c_bills%ROWCOUNT);
    CLOSE c_bills;
END;
/

4 cursor attributes:

AttributeReturnsUse
%FOUNDTRUE if last FETCH got a rowLoop condition
%NOTFOUNDTRUE if last FETCH got nothingEXIT WHEN
%ROWCOUNTNumber of rows fetched so farCount without COUNT()
%ISOPENTRUE if cursor is openSafety check before CLOSE

Pattern 15 — String / NULL handling (NVL, COALESCE, DECODE, CASE)

Shows up as "show 'No City' if city is null" or "categorize consumption as Low/Medium/High".

sql
-- NVL: replace NULL with a default
SELECT Cons_Name, NVL(City, 'No City') AS City FROM Consumer;

-- COALESCE: returns first non-null from a list
SELECT COALESCE(mobile, email, 'No Contact') AS Contact FROM Customer;

-- DECODE: Oracle's if-else inline (like a switch)
SELECT Bill_ID,
       DECODE(Status, 'PAID', 'Cleared', 'UNPAID', 'Pending', 'Unknown') AS Label
FROM Bill;

-- CASE: cleaner, standard SQL
SELECT Bill_ID, Units_Used,
       CASE
           WHEN Units_Used < 100  THEN 'Low'
           WHEN Units_Used < 400  THEN 'Medium'
           ELSE                        'High'
       END AS Consumption_Category
FROM Bill;

Probability map for your exam

PatternLikelihoodWhy
Trigger🔴 HighClassic PL/SQL exam staple
Nth highest🔴 HighExaminers love tripping people on this
Date filter🟡 MediumEasy to add to any schema
LEFT JOIN🟡 MediumTests JOIN knowledge explicitly
PROCEDURE/FUNCTION🟡 MediumNatural extension of PL/SQL block
CURSOR attributes🟠 Low-MediumUsually inside a bigger block
CASE/DECODE🟠 LowOften a sub-part of another query
SELF JOIN🟠 LowNeeds the right schema for it

Your 8 patterns cover the guaranteed marks. These 7 cover the "worst case" you asked about. Go through the trigger pattern carefully tonight — that's the most likely surprise. Good luck tomorrow.

You said: can you also add join unwind and other most important comparisions

can you also add join unwind and other most important comparisions

8:31 PM

Claude responded: Good call.

Good call. Let me cover the MongoDB side deeply since that's where most people lose marks.

MongoDB vs SQL — Complete Mapping

Core Concept Map

SQLMongoDBWhat it does
DatabaseDatabaseSame
TableCollectionWhere documents live
RowDocumentOne record (JSON)
ColumnFieldOne attribute
Primary Key_idAuto-created if not specified
JOIN$lookupLink two collections
GROUP BY$groupAggregate by field
WHERE$matchFilter documents
SELECT col$projectChoose which fields to show
ORDER BY$sortSort results
HAVING$match after $groupFilter on aggregated value
DISTINCT$group by that fieldUnique values
COUNT(*)$sum: 1Count documents
SUM(col)$sum: "$col"Sum a field
AVG(col)$avg: "$col"Average a field
MAX(col)$max: "$col"Maximum value
MIN(col)$min: "$col"Minimum value
LIMIT n$limit: nTop N results
SKIP n$skip: nPagination offset
LIKE '%x%'$regex: "x"Pattern match
IN (a,b,c)$in: [a,b,c]Match any of list
NOT IN$nin: [a,b,c]Match none of list
IS NULL$eq: nullField is null
IS NOT NULL$ne: nullField exists and not null
BETWEEN a AND b$gte: a, $lte: bRange filter
UPDATE SET$setUpdate specific fields
DELETE WHEREdeleteMany({filter})Remove matching docs
ALTER TABLE ADDNo schema — just insert with new fieldSchema-free

The Aggregation Pipeline — Deep Dive

Think of it exactly like a factory assembly line. Raw material (documents) enters stage 1, gets processed, output goes to stage 2, and so on. Each stage transforms the data.

javascript
db.collection.aggregate([
    { $stage1: { ... } },   // output of this feeds into
    { $stage2: { ... } },   // output of this feeds into
    { $stage3: { ... } }    // final output
]);

Stage 1 — $match (filter early, always put first)

javascript
// SQL: WHERE Status = 'UNPAID' AND Amount > 1000
db.bills.aggregate([
    { $match: { 
        Status: "UNPAID",
        Amount: { $gt: 1000 }
    }}
]);

// Comparison operators
$gt   // greater than        >
$gte  // greater or equal    >=
$lt   // less than           
$lte  // less or equal       <=
$eq   // equal               =
$ne   // not equal           !=
$in   // in list             IN (a,b,c)
$nin  // not in list         NOT IN (a,b,c)

Stage 2 — $group (the most important stage)

javascript
// SQL: SELECT City, COUNT(*), SUM(Amount), AVG(Units)
// FROM Bill GROUP BY City

db.bills.aggregate([
    { $group: {
        _id: "$City",              // GROUP BY City
        total_bills:  { $sum: 1 },         // COUNT(*)
        total_amount: { $sum: "$Amount" },  // SUM(Amount)
        avg_units:    { $avg: "$Units_Used" }, // AVG(Units_Used)
        max_amount:   { $max: "$Amount" },  // MAX(Amount)
        min_amount:   { $min: "$Amount" }   // MIN(Amount)
    }}
]);

_id in $group is mandatory — it's what you're grouping by. Set it to null if you want a global aggregate (like SELECT SUM(Amount) FROM Bill with no GROUP BY).

javascript
// Global total — no grouping
db.bills.aggregate([
    { $group: { _id: null, grand_total: { $sum: "$Amount" } } }
]);

Stage 3 — $project (choose and reshape fields)

javascript
// SQL: SELECT Cons_Name, Amount, Amount*0.18 AS Tax FROM Bill

db.bills.aggregate([
    { $project: {
        Cons_Name: 1,          // include (1 = show)
        Amount: 1,
        Tax: { $multiply: ["$Amount", 0.18] },  // computed field
        _id: 0                 // hide _id (0 = hide)
    }}
]);

Stage 4 — $sort and $limit

javascript
// SQL: ORDER BY Amount DESC FETCH FIRST 1 ROW ONLY
db.bills.aggregate([
    { $sort:  { Amount: -1 } },   // -1 = DESC, 1 = ASC
    { $limit: 1 }
]);

Stage 5 — $lookup (the JOIN)

This is what most students get wrong. $lookup links two collections like a LEFT JOIN.

javascript
// SQL:
// SELECT c.Cons_Name, b.Bill_Month, b.Amount
// FROM Consumer c
// LEFT JOIN Bill b ON c.Cons_ID = b.Cons_ID

db.consumer.aggregate([
    { $lookup: {
        from:         "bill",        // the other collection (right table)
        localField:   "Cons_ID",     // field in THIS collection (left)
        foreignField: "Cons_ID",     // field in OTHER collection (right)
        as:           "bills_data"   // name of the output array field
    }}
]);

Output shape — this is critical:

json
{
  "Cons_ID": 1,
  "Cons_Name": "Tarun",
  "City": "Madurai",
  "bills_data": [             // ← array of ALL matching bill documents
    { "Bill_ID": 1, "Amount": 1280, "Status": "PAID"   },
    { "Bill_ID": 2, "Amount": 1640, "Status": "UNPAID" }
  ]
}

The joined data comes back as a nested array inside each document. That's fundamentally different from SQL JOIN which flattens rows. This is where $unwind comes in.

Stage 6 — $unwind (flatten the array from $lookup)

After $lookup, each consumer document has an array of bills. $unwind explodes that array — one document per array element, like SQL JOIN's flat row output.

javascript
// Without $unwind: 1 consumer doc with array of 2 bills
// With $unwind:    2 flat docs, one per bill

db.consumer.aggregate([
    { $lookup: {
        from:         "bill",
        localField:   "Cons_ID",
        foreignField: "Cons_ID",
        as:           "bills_data"
    }},
    { $unwind: "$bills_data" }   // flatten the array
]);

Output after $unwind:

json
{ "Cons_Name": "Tarun", "bills_data": { "Bill_ID": 1, "Amount": 1280 } }
{ "Cons_Name": "Tarun", "bills_data": { "Bill_ID": 2, "Amount": 1640 } }

Now you can access bills_data.Amount, bills_data.Status etc. as flat fields in subsequent stages.

Full pipeline — $lookup + $unwind + $match + $group

This is the exam-level combination. "Find total amount paid per consumer":

javascript
db.consumer.aggregate([
    // Step 1: Join bills into each consumer
    { $lookup: {
        from: "bill", localField: "Cons_ID",
        foreignField: "Cons_ID", as: "bills_data"
    }},

    // Step 2: Flatten the array
    { $unwind: "$bills_data" },

    // Step 3: Filter only PAID bills
    { $match: { "bills_data.Status": "PAID" } },

    // Step 4: Group by consumer, sum amount
    { $group: {
        _id: "$Cons_Name",
        total_paid: { $sum: "$bills_data.Amount" }
    }},

    // Step 5: Sort highest first
    { $sort: { total_paid: -1 } }
]);

$unwind edge cases

javascript
// If a consumer has NO bills, $unwind removes them entirely (like INNER JOIN)
// To keep consumers with no bills (like LEFT JOIN):
{ $unwind: { 
    path: "$bills_data",
    preserveNullAndEmptyArrays: true   // keeps docs with empty array
}}

Complete MongoDB cheatsheet for exam

javascript
// INSERT
db.col.insertOne({ ... });
db.col.insertMany([{ ... }, { ... }]);

// SELECT *
db.col.find({});

// SELECT with WHERE
db.col.find({ Status: "UNPAID" });

// SELECT specific columns (projection)
db.col.find({ Status: "UNPAID" }, { Cons_Name: 1, Amount: 1, _id: 0 });

// UPDATE
db.col.updateOne(  { Bill_ID: 5 }, { $set: { Status: "PAID" } });
db.col.updateMany( { Status: "UNPAID" }, { $set: { Status: "PAID" } });

// DELETE
db.col.deleteOne(  { Bill_ID: 5 });
db.col.deleteMany( { Status: "UNPAID" });

// COUNT
db.col.countDocuments({ Status: "UNPAID" });

// DISTINCT
db.col.distinct("City");

// SORT + LIMIT
db.col.find({}).sort({ Amount: -1 }).limit(1);

// AGGREGATE pipeline stages in order:
// $match → $lookup → $unwind → $group → $project → $sort → $limit → $skip

The one rule that covers all pipeline questions

Stages run in order. Put $match first always — it reduces documents early so later stages process less data. Examiners sometimes ask "why do you put $match before $group" — answer is performance: filter first, then aggregate the smaller set.

You're covered now. Go get those marks tomorrow.

You said: and i will need some knowledge of basic operations in jdbc please do remember that we are the ones writing the query so don't sanitize the statements too much …

and i will need some knowledge of basic operations in jdbc please do remember that we are the ones writing the query so don't sanitize the statements too much only use prepared statements if necessary also add common mistakes i should not make in the exam while executing jdbc

8:34 PM

Claude responded: Good call covering JDBC.

Good call covering JDBC. Here's everything you need.

What JDBC Actually Is

JDBC is Java's way of talking to a database. Think of it like a phone call — you need to dial (connect), speak (send SQL), listen (get results), and hang up (close). The java.sql package provides all the classes for this.

The 5-step flow every JDBC program follows:

1. Load driver
2. Get connection
3. Create statement
4. Execute query
5. Close everything

Step 1 — Load Driver + Connect

java
import java.sql.*;

public class JDBCDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {

        // Load the Oracle JDBC driver
        Class.forName("oracle.jdbc.driver.OracleDriver");

        // Connect
        Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection(
            "jdbc:oracle:thin:@localhost:1521:XE",
            "system",
            "oracle"
        );

        System.out.println("Connected!");
    }
}

URL format — memorize this exactly:

jdbc:oracle:thin:@hostname:port:SID
jdbc:oracle:thin:@localhost:1521:XE

For MySQL (in case they ask):

jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/dbname
com.mysql.jdbc.Driver

Step 2 — Statement (use this for your own hardcoded queries)

Statement is for queries you write yourself with no user input. Since you're writing the SQL, no injection risk, no need for PreparedStatement.

java
Statement stmt = con.createStatement();

Three execution methods — know all three:

java
// 1. executeQuery() — for SELECT, returns ResultSet
ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery("SELECT * FROM Consumer");

// 2. executeUpdate() — for INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, returns int (rows affected)
int rows = stmt.executeUpdate("INSERT INTO Consumer VALUES (6,'Raj','TN111','Trichy')");
System.out.println(rows + " row(s) inserted.");

// 3. execute() — for anything, returns boolean (true if ResultSet, false if update count)
boolean hasRS = stmt.execute("CREATE TABLE Test (id NUMBER)");

Step 3 — Reading ResultSet

java
ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery("SELECT * FROM Consumer");

while (rs.next()) {
    // get by column index (starts at 1, NOT 0)
    int    id   = rs.getInt(1);
    String name = rs.getString(2);
    String city = rs.getString(3);

    // OR get by column name (safer to read)
    int    id2  = rs.getInt("Cons_ID");
    String name2= rs.getString("Cons_Name");

    System.out.println(id + " | " + name + " | " + city);
}

Step 4 — PreparedStatement (use when query has variables)

Only use this when a value needs to be substituted in. Parameters use ? as placeholder.

java
// Find bills for a specific consumer
PreparedStatement ps = con.prepareStatement(
    "SELECT * FROM Bill WHERE Cons_ID = ? AND Status = ?"
);

ps.setInt(1, 3);          // first  ? = Cons_ID
ps.setString(2, "UNPAID"); // second ? = Status

ResultSet rs = ps.executeQuery();

while (rs.next()) {
    System.out.println(rs.getInt("Bill_ID") + " - Rs." + rs.getDouble("Amount"));
}

Parameter setter methods:

java
ps.setInt(index, value)
ps.setString(index, value)
ps.setDouble(index, value)
ps.setDate(index, java.sql.Date.valueOf("2024-01-01"))

Step 5 — Full CRUD Example (what exams usually ask)

java
import java.sql.*;

public class BillingJDBC {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {

        Class.forName("oracle.jdbc.driver.OracleDriver");
        Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection(
            "jdbc:oracle:thin:@localhost:1521:XE", "system", "oracle"
        );
        con.setAutoCommit(false); // manual commit control

        Statement stmt = con.createStatement();

        // ── INSERT ───────────────────────────────────────────────
        int inserted = stmt.executeUpdate(
            "INSERT INTO Consumer VALUES (7, 'Vikram', 'TN777', 'Coimbatore')"
        );
        System.out.println("Inserted: " + inserted + " row(s)");

        // ── SELECT ───────────────────────────────────────────────
        ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery(
            "SELECT Cons_ID, Cons_Name, City FROM Consumer"
        );
        System.out.println("\nAll Consumers:");
        while (rs.next()) {
            System.out.println(
                rs.getInt("Cons_ID") + " | " +
                rs.getString("Cons_Name") + " | " +
                rs.getString("City")
            );
        }

        // ── UPDATE ───────────────────────────────────────────────
        int updated = stmt.executeUpdate(
            "UPDATE Bill SET Status = 'PAID' WHERE Bill_ID = 2"
        );
        System.out.println("\nUpdated: " + updated + " row(s)");

        // ── DELETE ───────────────────────────────────────────────
        int deleted = stmt.executeUpdate(
            "DELETE FROM Consumer WHERE Cons_ID = 7"
        );
        System.out.println("Deleted: " + deleted + " row(s)");

        con.commit(); // commit all changes
        System.out.println("Committed.");

        // ── CLOSE ────────────────────────────────────────────────
        rs.close();
        stmt.close();
        con.close();
    }
}

Step 6 — JDBC with TCL (Transactions)

java
con.setAutoCommit(false);  // turn off auto-commit first

try {
    stmt.executeUpdate("INSERT INTO Bill VALUES (12, 1, 'MAR-2024', 300, 700, 'UNPAID')");
    stmt.executeUpdate("UPDATE Consumer SET City = 'Chennai' WHERE Cons_ID = 1");

    con.commit();   // both succeed — commit
    System.out.println("Transaction committed.");

} catch (SQLException e) {
    con.rollback(); // any failure — rollback everything
    System.out.println("Transaction rolled back: " + e.getMessage());
}

With Savepoint:

java
con.setAutoCommit(false);

stmt.executeUpdate("INSERT INTO Bill VALUES (13, 2, 'MAR-2024', 200, 500, 'UNPAID')");

Savepoint sp = con.setSavepoint("after_insert");  // bookmark

stmt.executeUpdate("UPDATE Bill SET Amount = 9999 WHERE Bill_ID = 999");

con.rollback(sp);   // undo only the update, insert stays
con.commit();       // commit the insert

System.out.println("Partial rollback done.");

Step 7 — ResultSetMetaData (bonus, sometimes asked)

"Print column names dynamically without hardcoding them."

java
ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery("SELECT * FROM Consumer");
ResultSetMetaData md = rs.getMetaData();

int cols = md.getColumnCount();

// Print headers
for (int i = 1; i <= cols; i++) {
    System.out.print(md.getColumnName(i) + "\t");
}
System.out.println();

// Print rows
while (rs.next()) {
    for (int i = 1; i <= cols; i++) {
        System.out.print(rs.getString(i) + "\t");
    }
    System.out.println();
}

Common Exam Mistakes — Do NOT do these

Mistake 1 — Column index starts at 0

java
// WRONG
rs.getString(0);  // throws SQLException

// RIGHT
rs.getString(1);  // first column is 1

Mistake 2 — Calling rs.next() wrong

java
// WRONG — skips first row
rs.next();
while (rs.next()) { ... }

// RIGHT
while (rs.next()) { ... }

Mistake 3 — Using executeQuery for INSERT/UPDATE

java
// WRONG — executeQuery expects a ResultSet, INSERT returns none
ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery("INSERT INTO ...");

// RIGHT
int rows = stmt.executeUpdate("INSERT INTO ...");

Mistake 4 — Using executeUpdate for SELECT

java
// WRONG
int n = stmt.executeUpdate("SELECT * FROM Consumer");

// RIGHT
ResultSet rs = stmt.executeQuery("SELECT * FROM Consumer");

Mistake 5 — Forgetting setAutoCommit(false) for transactions

java
// If you don't set this, every statement auto-commits immediately
// con.rollback() does nothing because it's already committed
con.setAutoCommit(false); // always set this before transaction blocks

Mistake 6 — Closing in wrong order

java
// WRONG — closing connection first invalidates stmt and rs
con.close();
rs.close();
stmt.close();

// RIGHT — close innermost first
rs.close();
stmt.close();
con.close();

Mistake 7 — Forgetting Class.forName

java
// Without this, JVM doesn't know which JDBC driver to use
// Throws: No suitable driver found
Class.forName("oracle.jdbc.driver.OracleDriver"); // don't skip this

Mistake 8 — Wrong SID in URL

java
// XE is the SID for Oracle Express Edition
// Using ORCL (full Oracle) instead of XE when on Express will fail
"jdbc:oracle:thin:@localhost:1521:XE"  // for Oracle XE

Mistake 9 — Not importing java.sql.*

java
// Every JDBC program needs this — examiners check for it
import java.sql.*;

Mistake 10 — Forgetting throws Exception or try-catch

java
// JDBC methods throw checked exceptions — won't compile without handling
public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception { ... }
// OR wrap everything in try-catch

Quick Reference Card

TaskMethodReturns
SELECTexecuteQuery("SELECT...")ResultSet
INSERT/UPDATE/DELETEexecuteUpdate("...")int (rows affected)
DDL (CREATE/DROP)execute("...")boolean
Next rowrs.next()boolean
Get int columnrs.getInt(1) or rs.getInt("col")int
Get string columnrs.getString(1)String
Get double columnrs.getDouble(1)double
Commitcon.commit()void
Rollbackcon.rollback()void
Savepointcon.setSavepoint("name")Savepoint
Rollback to savecon.rollback(sp)void

You're covered. Post your previous batch questions whenever you're ready.
